description: Handle collections of data efficiently using arrays and manipulate text with C strings.

slides.title: Data Collections
slides.content:
# Arrays 📚

What if you need to store the marks of 100 students? Declaring 100 variables (`mark1`, `mark2`...) is crazy!

**Arrays** solve this. An array is a collection of variables of the **same type** stored in contiguous memory locations.

## Syntax
```c
type name[size];
int marks[5]; // Stores 5 integers
```

slides.title: Indices and Initialization
Index Check
slides.content: For `int arr[10];`, what is the index of the last element?

slides.title: Multidimensional Arrays
Introduction to Strings
slides.content:
# Text in C 🔤

C does **not** have a native `string` type. Instead, strings are **arrays of characters** terminated by a special **Null Character `\0`**.

```c
char name[6] = {'H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', '\0'};
// OR easier way:
char name[] = "Hello"; // Compiler adds \0 automatically
```

The `\0` (ASCII 0) tells functions where the string ends.

slides.title: String I/O
slides.content:
# Reading and Printing Strings 🎤

## Printing
Use `%s` specifier.
```c
printf("Hello %s", name);
```

## Reading
`scanf` stops at whitespace (space, tab, newline).
To read a full line, use `fgets`.

```c
char fullName[50];
printf("Enter full name: ");
fgets(fullName, sizeof(fullName), stdin);
```
*(Avoid `gets()` as it is unsafe and causes buffer overflows!)*

slides.title: String Library
slides.content:
# Helper Functions 🛠️

Include `<string.h>` for powerful tools.

1.  **`strlen(s)`:** Returns length (excluding `\0`).
2.  **`strcpy(dest, src)`:** Copies src to dest.
3.  **`strcat(dest, src)`:** Appends src to start of dest.
4.  **`strcmp(s1, s2)`:** Compares two strings.
    - 0 if equal
    - <0 if s1 < s2
    - >0 if s1 > s2

> ⚠️ **You cannot use `=`** to assign strings after declaration! Use `strcpy`.

slides.title: Full Code Example
Summary
slides.content:
# Recap: Arrays & Strings 🎓

Arrays allow you to manage bulk data efficiently.

## Key Takeaways
- **Declaration:** `type name[size]`. Size is fixed.
- **Access:** 0-based index. `arr[last]` is out of bounds!
- **2D Arrays:** Rows and Columns `arr[r][c]`.
- **Strings:** Character arrays ending with `\0`.
- **String Lib:** Use `<string.h>` functions (`strcpy`, `strlen`) instead of operators like `=` or `==`.

Arrays are closely related to **Pointers**, which is our next topic!
